content.text: Bridge Call and War Room Setup Protocols

When a high-severity incident strikes, the difference between a 20-minute resolution and a 4-hour outage often comes down to whether the response team was organized within the first five minutes. Bridge call and war room protocols define how people connect, who leads, what information flows where, and how decisions get made under pressure. Without these protocols, incident responders talk over each other, duplicate work, lose track of hypotheses, and leave stakeholders in the dark.
Core Concept

A war room is not a meeting room. It is a structured coordination space—virtual or physical—with defined roles, a shared information surface, a decision log, and strict communication discipline. The bridge call is the real-time voice or video layer of that space. Together they form the command-and-control backbone of incident response.
The key principle:
separate the coordination channel from the investigation channel

. The bridge call handles role clarification, status updates, and decisions. The war room Slack channel captures the working log: hypotheses, metrics screenshots, runbook links, and timeline entries. Mixing the two causes noise, missed actions, and confusion about what was decided versus what was merely discussed.
Architecture: Roles and Communication Channels

attrs.alt: War room role structure: IC, SME, Comms Lead, Exec Sponsor, shared Slack channel
attrs.title: War Room Communication Architecture

content.text: War Room Roles
Every participant in a war room must hold exactly one primary role. People without a role should not be in the bridge call—they create noise without contributing signal.
Incident Commander (IC)
Runs the bridge call: speaks first, controls airtime, closes discussions with a decision
Tracks the action item table in the war room doc in real time
Does NOT diagnose or fix—delegates all technical work to SMEs
Calls 30-minute status checkpoints and drives the update to stakeholders
Owns the decision to escalate, roll back, or declare resolved
Subject Matter Experts (SMEs)
On-call engineers from the services implicated in the incident
Investigate, propose hypotheses, run tests, and execute mitigations
Report findings to the IC with a clear confidence level: suspected / confirmed / ruled out
Log findings and commands in the war room channel as they work
Communications Lead
Posts status page updates on the cadence defined by severity (every 15 min for SEV-1, 30 min for SEV-2)
Drafts internal stakeholder messages for the IC to review before sending
Monitors customer support channels for new reports that change scope understanding
Executive Sponsor (when paged)
Authorizes resource expenditure beyond normal on-call scope (vendor emergency support, team page-outs)
Makes customer notification decisions and regulatory disclosure calls
Listens silently on the bridge; speaks only when IC requests input
Bridge Call Setup

The bridge call link must be established before the incident happens. A pre-generated, severity-specific conference line (Zoom/Meet/Teams) avoids the 90-second delay of generating a new meeting link during a crisis. Most teams maintain two permanent war room links: one for SEV-1 and one for SEV-2.
Bridge call opening script (IC delivers at T+0):

"This is [IC name] running incident response for SEV-[N] declared at [time].
Symptom: [one sentence].
Current hypothesis: [working theory or 'unknown'].
Role check: SME [name] for [service]—confirmed? Comms lead [name]—confirmed?
Action item 1: [SME] investigate [X] and report back in 10 minutes.
I will give a status update at [time+30min]. All hypotheses and commands in #inc-channel.
Question?"
War Room Channel Setup
Create a dedicated Slack channel using a consistent naming scheme. The name must encode enough context to be bookmarked and searched after the incident.

# Channel naming convention
# Format: #inc-{severity}-{YYYYMMDD}-{short-slug}
# Examples:
#inc-sev1-20260714-llm-api-timeout
#inc-sev2-20260714-prompt-cache-miss-rate

# Pin these resources in the channel on creation:
# 1. War room doc link (pre-templated)
# 2. Bridge call link
# 3. Monitoring dashboard link
# 4. Runbook link (if known)
# 5. Incident ticket number

The war room document (Google Doc or Notion page) serves as the single authoritative record. Open the template before the bridge call, not during. Teams that spend 5 minutes finding and formatting the doc during a live SEV-1 waste critical investigation time.
War Room Document Template
A war room document must be writable by all responders simultaneously and must contain four sections:
Timeline
: timestamped entries of events, actions taken, and findings. Format: [HH:MM UTC] [who] [what]. This becomes the postmortem source of truth.
Hypothesis Log
: each theory with status (active/confirmed/ruled-out), evidence for, evidence against, and next investigation step.
Action Items
: owner, description, target time, and status (open/in-progress/done). IC updates this in real time during the bridge call.
Decisions
: every decision the IC makes, with the rationale and who was consulted. Prevents relitigating decisions mid-incident.
Bridge Call Discipline
Bridge calls degrade rapidly without explicit rules. The following constraints are non-negotiable during an active SEV-1:
Mute unless speaking.
Background noise from unmuted participants degrades comprehension under stress.
Speak to the IC, not to the group.
All communications flow through the IC; cross-talk fragments attention.
State confidence levels.
'I think' vs. 'I confirmed' are different signal strengths that affect IC decision-making.
No parallel problem-solving on the call.
Investigation happens in side threads; the bridge call hears results only.
IC controls the clock.
If a discussion runs over 3 minutes without producing a decision, the IC calls it: 'We're parking this. Action item to [person] to investigate offline.'
Automation: War Room Bootstrap Script
Manual war room setup introduces 3–7 minutes of delay. Automate the bootstrap through a Slack bot or CLI tool triggered by the incident management system:

import re
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class WarRoomConfig:
    severity: int          # 1 or 2
    slug: str              # short-slug, e.g. 'llm-api-timeout'
    ic: str                # Slack user ID
    sme_ids: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    comms_id: Optional[str] = None

    @property
    def channel_name(self) -> str:
        date = datetime.now(timezone.utc).strftime('%Y%m%d')
        safe_slug = re.sub(r'[^a-z0-9-]', '-', self.slug.lower())[:30]
        return f'inc-sev{self.severity}-{date}-{safe_slug}'

    @property
    def bridge_url(self) -> str:
        # Pre-created permanent war room meetings per severity
        return {
            1: 'https://meet.company.com/war-room-sev1',
            2: 'https://meet.company.com/war-room-sev2',
        }[self.severity]


def bootstrap_war_room(config: WarRoomConfig, slack_client) -> dict:
    """Create channel, invite roles, pin resources, post opening."""
    # 1. Create channel
    channel = slack_client.conversations_create(name=config.channel_name)
    channel_id = channel['channel']['id']

    # 2. Invite all participants
    all_users = [config.ic] + config.sme_ids
    if config.comms_id:
        all_users.append(config.comms_id)
    slack_client.conversations_invite(channel=channel_id, users=','.join(all_users))

    # 3. Post opening message with role assignments
    opening = (
        f'*War Room Open — SEV-{config.severity}*
'
        f'IC: <@{config.ic}>
'
        f'SMEs: {" ".join(f"<@{s}>" for s in config.sme_ids)}
'
        f'Bridge: {config.bridge_url}

'
        f'Post timeline entries as: 
'
        f'Report to IC before changing anything in production.'
    )
    slack_client.chat_postMessage(channel=channel_id, text=opening)

    return {'channel_id': channel_id, 'channel_name': config.channel_name}
Tooling for War Rooms
War room tooling spans four categories. Teams often discover during a live incident that they lack permissions or haven't pre-configured a tool:
Category
Common Tooling
Pre-Incident Requirement
Voice/Video
Zoom, Google Meet, Teams
Permanent meeting links per severity; all IC-eligible engineers have host access
Chat/Channel
Slack, Teams
Bot with auto-channel-create; on-call has channel-create permission
Document
Google Docs, Notion, Confluence
Template with auto-title; link in channel bot message
Incident Ticket
PagerDuty, Jira Service Mgmt, Opsgenie
Linked to Slack channel; severity auto-assigns IC from rotation
Monitoring
Datadog, Grafana, Honeycomb
Shared dashboard links per service; read-only guest accounts for execs
Common Failure Modes
No IC designated in first 5 minutes.
Everyone investigates independently, nobody coordinates. Mitigate with on-call policy: the engineer who acknowledges the page is the acting IC until a trained IC joins.
Too many people in the bridge call.
Observers should join only the Slack channel; the bridge call stays to active role holders plus direct contributors. Cap at 8 people on the bridge.
Decisions made in DMs or side threads.
Any decision that affects the incident must be logged in the war room channel. Shadow coordination produces contradictory actions.
Stale timeline.
If responders don't log as they work, the postmortem timeline must be reconstructed from memory—inaccurate and incomplete. Enforce 'log first, then act' discipline.
GenAI-Specific Considerations
Incidents in GenAI systems present additional war room challenges that don't arise in purely deterministic services:
Output quality degradation may not page.
Users see wrong or harmful outputs without any error metric firing. The comms lead must monitor user feedback and support channels as a secondary signal.
LLM provider war rooms.
For incidents in third-party model APIs (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.), the SME role expands to include a vendor escalation contact. Establish vendor bridge call access before incidents occur.
Prompt-related incidents need eval evidence.
SMEs should run offline eval suites against suspected prompt changes to provide confirmed vs. suspected classification to the IC, not just anecdotal examples.
Safety incidents require legal review before comms.

If the incident involves harmful model outputs, route all external communications through legal review. The Comms Lead must not post status page updates about content safety issues without explicit sign-off.
Protocol Runbook Excerpt

# war-room-protocol.yaml - checked into incident-runbooks repo
war_room:
  activation_trigger: SEV-1 or SEV-2 declared
  auto_bootstrap:
    tool: warroom-bot
    slack_command: /incident sev1 llm-api-timeout
    creates:
      - slack_channel: '#inc-sev1-{date}-{slug}'
      - war_room_doc: from template 'incident-war-room-v3'
      - pagerduty_incident: linked to channel
  bridge_call:
    sev1_link: https://meet.company.com/war-room-sev1
    sev2_link: https://meet.company.com/war-room-sev2
    opening_script: runbooks/bridge-call-script.md
    max_participants: 8
  update_cadence:
    sev1: 15 minutes
    sev2: 30 minutes
  roles:
    ic:
      source: pagerduty_ic_rotation
      fallback: on-call engineer who acknowledged
    sme:
      source: service on-call rotation for implicated services
    comms:
      source: comms on-call (business hours) | ic-backup (after-hours)
    exec_sponsor:
      trigger: sev1 active > 45 minutes or customer data impact
  channel_discipline:
    - All hypotheses logged in channel before testing
    - All commands run in production logged in channel
    - IC approval required before any production change
    - Decisions table updated after each IC ruling
Key Takeaways
War room protocols must be automated. Manual channel creation and document setup during a live incident is unacceptable—every second counts in SEV-1.
Roles must be explicit. Every person in the war room has one named role. The IC leads, SMEs investigate, Comms handles stakeholders, Exec sponsors authorize. No role blending.
The timeline is sacred. Everything goes in the war room doc's timeline. This document becomes the postmortem source of truth and must not be reconstructed from memory.
Practice the protocol before the incident. Run war room simulations quarterly so the setup process is muscle memory. Responders should not be reading a setup guide during a production outage.
